steps.title: Reading Distance–Time Graphs
steps.content:
On a **distance–time graph**:

- A **horizontal line** = stationary (not moving)
- A **straight diagonal line** = constant speed
- A **curved line** = changing speed (accelerating or decelerating)

The **gradient** (slope) of the line equals the speed:

speed = gradient = Δd / Δt

steps.title: Distance–Time Graphs
steps.question: A distance–time graph shows a straight line with a steeper gradient in section B than section A. What does this tell you?
options: The object is moving faster in section B than section A
The object is accelerating in section B
The object is decelerating in section B
The object is stationary in section A
steps.correctFeedback: Correct! A steeper gradient means a greater change in distance per unit time — so the object is moving faster. Acceleration would be shown by a curve, not a straight line.
steps.misconceptionFeedback: On a d–t graph, gradient = speed. A steeper straight line means faster constant speed. A curve would mean acceleration. Two straight sections with different gradients = two different constant speeds.
